metadata.title: Workflow Card: Saved Prompt vs. Reusable Recipe

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Her fourth run broke because an archived subfolder appeared and the same prompt processed six-month-old notes, and the fix was the 'when not to use' and stop-condition fields a bare prompt never had. I want to turn one of my own repeated prompts into a full workflow card. Help me (1) fill all eight fields including explicit input paths and excluded folders, (2) write a 'when not to use' field naming the conditions that silently held on the first run, and (3) write stop conditions a human monitoring the run should watch for, like a count mismatch or a file from an unlisted path.

props.command: Her fourth run broke because an archived subfolder appeared and the same prompt processed six-month-old notes, and the fix was the 'when not to use' and stop-condition fields a bare prompt never had. I want to turn one of my own repeated prompts into a full workflow card. Help me (1) fill all eight fields including explicit input paths and excluded folders, (2) write a 'when not to use' field naming the conditions that silently held on the first run, and (3) write stop conditions a human monitoring the run should watch for, like a count mismatch or a file from an unlisted path.
props.segment: Workflow Card: Saved Prompt vs. Reusable

beats.narration_text: She had run the weekly status report task three times with the same prompt. Fourth run: same prompt, different month. The shared folder had an archived subfolder now. The task processed everything — the current week's notes and the archived ones from six months ago. The output was longer, more confused, and wrong. The prompt was the same. The conditions had changed. A saved prompt is one ingredient. A workflow card is the whole recipe. This is Liam, in for Bear.
beats.new_visual_element: ClaudeComposerAsk — cold open, run status report from saved prompt

props.command: run the weekly status report workflow

beats.narration_text: A saved prompt captures what to do. A workflow card captures what to do, what inputs to include, what to exclude, what tools are allowed, what the verification steps are, when not to use the workflow, and what conditions cause a stop. The 'when not to use' field is what breaks most reuse attempts. The original task was safe because certain conditions held. Without the card, those conditions are invisible on the second run.
beats.new_visual_element: Prompt vs. card — prompt: what to do / card: + inputs / exclusions / when-not / stop conditions

beats.narration_text: The workflow card has eight fields: purpose, inputs with explicit paths, excluded files or folders, allowed tools, the task brief, verification steps after the run, stop conditions — the conditions under which you halt before completing — and a handoff note for anyone else who runs it. The stop condition field is the hardest to write and the most important. It forces you to think about what could go wrong before it does.
beats.new_visual_element: Eight card fields — purpose / inputs / exclusions / tools / brief / verify / stop / handoff

beats.narration_text: The stop condition is what a human monitoring the task should see that causes them to halt the run before it completes. An unexpected file type in the input. A count mismatch. A file from a path not in the inputs list. A document that references a personnel matter. The stop condition is a concrete, observable trigger — not a general 'use judgment' instruction. Writing it down is what makes the oversight concrete rather than aspirational.
beats.new_visual_element: Stop condition examples — unexpected file type / count mismatch / unlisted path access

beats.narration_text: The workflow card is the artifact that makes the second run as safe as the first. It captures the tacit knowledge from the original run — the things you would have told a colleague before they took over. When not to use it. What the scope exclusions are. What to do if the run produces something unexpected. Without the card, the second run inherits the conditions of the first without the understanding of why those conditions mattered.
beats.new_visual_element: Artifact view — card makes second run inherit first run's safety, not just its prompt

beats.narration_text: Build the card for a workflow you have already run. Paste this.

props.command: Help me build a workflow card for a Cowork task I have run before. The task: [describe it]. Walk me through these eight fields: (1) purpose, (2) inputs with explicit paths, (3) excluded files or folders, (4) allowed tools, (5) task brief, (6) verification steps, (7) stop conditions — observable triggers to halt the run, (8) handoff note for someone else running it. Ask me for each field I skip.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Workflow Card: Saved Prompt vs. Reusable Recipe] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
